narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 60.5% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. You have some standout strengths, like your impressive LSAT score of 46—well above the typical 36.7 average—and a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50, higher than the usual 3.21. However, your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3, both at 3.0, are notably lower than the averages of about 5.7 and 5.5, placing you in the lower third of your peers academically. Your upper-middle family income aligns closely with typical applicants, and as a white male planning full-time studies, your demographics match the majority in our dataset—white students make up 84%, males 56%, and full-time enrollment 93%. When the model evaluated your profile, your Year 3 decile ranking had the strongest influence, pushing the prediction toward a higher risk of bar failure despite your other positives. Next in impact was your LSAT score, which surprisingly also nudged the odds toward failure in this context, followed closely by your Year 1 decile. Your family income level, undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status rounded out the influences, each contributing modestly toward the same concerning direction, though with decreasing weight. Overall, your lower school deciles stood out as the top drivers compared to peers who typically rank higher and pass the bar more reliably. This means, relative to successful applicants, your early and later school performance signaled potential challenges in sustaining the rigor needed for the bar. To improve your future chances, focus on boosting your academic deciles through stronger performance in remaining coursework—aiming for top-half rankings could shift predictions dramatically. You might also consider targeted bar exam prep programs proven to help students with similar profiles, or even retaking the LSAT if you can push it even higher. With dedication to these academic steps, you could reapply stronger next cycle and flip the model's outlook in your favor. We're rooting for your success.
